---
title: "Overview"
---

How can a researcher request only the specific data they need, in a reproducible
and machine-readable way, without requiring the data owner to interpret long
emails and manually produce a query for such requests?

Meet Propagate, a tool for creating, exchanging, and executing data subset
requests against structured datasets. Propagate mediates between *requesters*
(researchers/analysts who want data) and *owners* (data managers who control the
data) and provides a standardized way to define dataset requests from a larger
pool of data.

Propagate is essentially a requesision system: A *requester* creates
instructions containing e.g., which variables/columns they need, which
tables/resources they need, and which row filters should be applied.
Additionally, the *requester* provides *project metadata* (who they are, why
they need the data). The *owner* then uses those instructions together with the
metadata from the available data to generate a reproducible subset.

## Why use Propagate?

The overall aim of the [Seedcase Project](https://seedcase-project.org) is to
build tools that make data more
[FAIR](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/), easier to access and share,
and more likely to be (re)used. To support this goal, Propagate solves two
critical issues.

*Data requesting* Allow a researcher to create subset requests interactively
through a website, which hosts the dataset metadata. Based on this information,
the requester can select columns and filters visually, and export or submit a
request for accessing the (sub)-dataset.

*Data curation* Allow a data owner to execute those requests without a server
infrastructure required. The format guarantees reproducible execution and
provides validation against metadata to generate the new data resource (a
.parquet file).

Propagate improves the accessibility of data, which helps with making data
(re)use more appealing for requesters and less of a dauting task for owners.
This is an important part in making data FAIR, as the notion of providing data
upon "reasonable request" has, paradoxically, turned into an inside joke and a
meme in the scientific community. Propagate aims to formalize and theirby lower
the friction of sharing data.

Propagate fills a special role within the scientific community, as few tools are
available that mediate between data requesters and data owners.

## The tools of Propagate:

Propagate has two tools; a *web app* for the data requester and a *CLI* for the
data owner.

The Web app reads metadata and displays available resources and variables. From
this, it allows column selection, row filtering, and simple table joins. It also
collects project information and generates and requesition file that can be
saved/exported/sent to the data owner.

The CLI app is only used by the data owner and helps with reading the
requesition file, validating it against metadata, and converting the request
into executable query that generates the new data resource as a .parquet file.
Propagate supports [Flower](https://flower.seedcase-project.org).

## Learning more

This website contains documentation on Propagates's design, how to use it, and
details about the interface.

- [How-to guide](/docs/guide/index.qmd): The guide section provides a
  step-by-step introduction to Propagate, including installing, using the
  website app, using the command-line interface, and setting configurations.
- [Reference](/docs/reference/index.qmd): The reference section contains
  detailed information about Propagate's CLI and setting up a Propagate website
  for requesters.
- [Design](/docs/design/index.qmd): The design section describes the
  architecture and interface of Propagate, the requirements, use-cases, and [C4
  model](https://c4model.com/) diagrams.

## Contributors

{{< include /docs/includes/_contributors.qmd >}}
